findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a statement to the Security section specifying whether the cooldown timestamp is updated on a failed JWKS fetch, with explicit rationale for whichever policy is chosen (e.g. 'update on failure to preserve the flood guard; operators experiencing persistent outages should extend the cooldown or pre-warm the cache').
evidence: Plan: 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises (composite treats as non-acceptance → deny)'
Plan: 'caps refetch on an unknown kid to at most one refetch per auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown seconds'
No statement in the plan about whether the cooldown timestamp is recorded on a failed fetch
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether the cooldown timestamp is updated when a JWKS fetch fails. This leaves an unresolved edge case: if the timestamp IS updated on failure, a single transient JWKS-endpoint error will block all token validation for the entire cooldown window (availability DoS from infrastructure hiccup); if it is NOT updated, an attacker flooding unknown kids that cause fetch failures can trigger unbounded JWKS fetches (the flood-guard goal fails on error paths). The correct behavior is non-obvious and materially affects both security and availability.
findings.location: Security — 'Key rotation without DoS (concurrency-safe)' / 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises'
scenarios: JWKS endpoint returns a 503 for 5 seconds; with cooldown-on-failure, all tokens are rejected for 30 s after the endpoint recovers
Attacker floods random kids; JWKS endpoint returns errors; without cooldown-on-failure, the flood guard fails and every burst triggers a new fetch attempt
findings.suggested_fix: Update on failure (record the timestamp even when the fetch fails) to preserve the flood-guard property; then document this explicitly so operators understand a transient JWKS outage can extend the rejection window up to one cooldown period.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that a failed JWKS fetch does NOT advance the cooldown timestamp (so the next request can immediately retry), and document this in the Security section and the corresponding unit test.
evidence: 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises (composite treats as non-acceptance → deny)'
'the verifier caps refetch on an unknown kid to at most one refetch per auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown seconds'
Plan is silent on whether a failed refetch consumes the cooldown budget, leaving a TOCTOU between 'fetch failed' and 'timestamp updated'.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether a failed JWKS fetch updates the cooldown timestamp. If it does, a single transient JWKS-endpoint error sets the last-refetch mark, blocking all retry attempts for the full cooldown window (default 30 s) while every JWT-authenticated request is denied — an inadvertent denial-of-service on a transient error.
findings.location: Security section — 'Key rotation without DoS' + 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises'
scenarios: JWKS endpoint has a transient 500 or network blip; the first request triggers a fetch, it fails, the cooldown timestamp is stamped, and every subsequent request for the next 30 s is denied without attempting a refetch — all valid tokens are rejected for 30 s on a single transient error.
findings.suggested_fix: In the cooldown logic: only update `_last_refetch_at` after a successful JWKS fetch. If the fetch raises, propagate the exception without touching the timestamp, so the next request can attempt a refetch immediately.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly in the Security section whether the cooldown timestamp is updated *before* the JWKS fetch is dispatched (optimistic, inside the Lock) or *after* it completes (post-fetch), and state the chosen behavior for fetch failures (i.e., does a failed refetch still update the timestamp?).
evidence: Plan text: 'the cooldown's read-check-then-update of the last-refetch timestamp is a check-then-act (TOCTOU) hazard; it is therefore made atomic under an asyncio.Lock that serializes the check-and-update'
The plan never specifies whether the timestamp update occurs before or after `asyncio.to_thread(get_signing_key_from_jwt)`.
AC bullet 8 asserts 'at most one JWKS fetch' but this guarantee depends on the timestamp being written before the fetch — if written after, two concurrent coroutines can both pass the check.
findings.finding: The cooldown state-increment placement for the `asyncio.Lock`-guarded last-refetch timestamp is not specified: the plan says 'check-and-update' but never states whether the timestamp is written *before* the JWKS fetch (preventing concurrent fetches but charging the cooldown on failure) or *after* the fetch (allowing concurrent fetches if two coroutines read the stale timestamp before either writes). This placement is load-bearing for both the correctness guarantee and the AC 8 observable oracle.
findings.location: Security section — 'it is therefore made atomic under an asyncio.Lock that serializes the check-and-update'; Approach section — 'serializes the cooldown check-and-update with an asyncio.Lock'
scenarios: If timestamp is written after the fetch completes, two coroutines racing on the same unknown kid can both read the stale (zero/old) timestamp inside separate Lock acquisitions, both decide 'cooldown elapsed', and both launch a fetch — defeating the flood guard.
If timestamp is written before the fetch, a transient fetch failure permanently charges the cooldown window.
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: 'The timestamp is updated inside the Lock *before* dispatching `asyncio.to_thread(...)` so concurrent coroutines see the updated mark and skip the fetch. A failed fetch does NOT reset the timestamp — the cooldown window remains charged to prevent retry storms.'

findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens to the cooldown timestamp when a JWKS fetch FAILS (raises). If the timestamp IS updated on failure, an attacker flooding one unknown kid can lock out legitimate key-rotation refetches for 30 s; if it is NOT updated, a flood of failing requests (e.g., JWKS endpoint down) can each trigger a new blocked-thread attempt every request cycle, defeating the DoS guard.
findings.location: Security section — 'Key rotation without DoS (concurrency-safe)'; Acceptance Criteria — unknown-kid cooldown test
findings.suggested_fix: Recommended policy: update the cooldown timestamp on fetch failure (rate-limit failed fetches the same as successful ones), and document that legitimate rotation after an outage will be picked up on the next post-cooldown request. This keeps the DoS guard intact at the cost of a maximum 30 s recovery delay.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the full closed set of accepted asymmetric algorithm strings (e.g. `RS256`, `RS384`, `RS512`, `PS256`, `PS384`, `PS512`, `ES256`, `ES384`, `ES512`, `EdDSA`) in the Security section alongside the symmetric rejection set, so the `algorithms=` allowlist and startup-refusal check have an unambiguous, testable vocabulary.
evidence: Plan text: 'anything outside the asymmetric families `RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA` on a JWKS source is likewise refused'
The symmetric closed set IS specified: '{HS256, HS384, HS512}', but the asymmetric allowlist is not — creating an asymmetry in specification quality.
AC bullet 6 tests startup refusal for HS256 but not for an algorithm outside both sets (e.g. a novel string), leaving the boundary underspecified.
findings.finding: The asymmetric algorithm allowlist is expressed as wildcard families (`RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA`) but the exact closed string set is never enumerated. An executor cannot implement the startup-refusal check or the `algorithms=` allowlist from wildcards alone — they must independently decide which specific strings (e.g. RS256/RS384/RS512, PS256/PS384/PS512, ES256/ES384/ES512, EdDSA) are valid, making the allowed set ambiguous and untestable.
findings.location: Security section — 'anything outside the asymmetric families RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA on a JWKS source is likewise refused'
scenarios: An executor might include or exclude EdDSA, or include ES384/ES512 vs only ES256, leading to provider incompatibilities.
A test for 'unknown algorithm not in any family' cannot be written without knowing the exact closed set.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'anything outside the asymmetric families `RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA`' with an explicit closed list: `{RS256, RS384, RS512, PS256, PS384, PS512, ES256, ES384, ES512, EdDSA}` (case-sensitive, matching PyJWT's algorithm name strings).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `scope`/`scp` precedence rule (e.g., 'prefer `scope` if present; fall back to `scp`; if both present, use `scope`') co-located with the scope-mapping description in the 'What' section, and add an AC test asserting a JWT carrying only `scp` produces the expected `AccessToken.scopes`.
evidence: Plan text: 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes'
No AC bullet covers the `scp` claim path at all — the only AC for scopes covers a token with 'correct iss/aud(=resource) → AccessToken with the expected scopes'.
No processing rule specifies precedence (scope > scp, scp > scope, or union) when both claims coexist.
findings.finding: The `scope`/`scp`→scopes dual-claim mapping is specified as a feature but the decision rule for when both claims are present simultaneously is never defined. An executor does not know whether to take `scope` first, `scp` first, or merge them (union/intersection), and this rule is not co-located with the `AccessToken.scopes` output description.
findings.location: What section — 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes'
scenarios: A JWT from Azure AD may carry both `scope` (space-separated) and `scp` (array) with different contents; the executor must pick one without guidance.
A test using the `scp` claim variant will produce different results depending on which claim wins.

findings.finding: The closed set of accepted asymmetric algorithm strings is specified only as wildcard families ('RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA') but the exact strings an executor must allow are never enumerated. The symmetric-rejection set IS explicitly enumerated ({HS256, HS384, HS512}), but the allowed-asymmetric side is not — an executor must independently decide whether 'RS*' includes RS256/RS384/RS512/RS1 and whether 'PS*' includes PS256/PS384/PS512. This is a vocabulary-completeness gap (T8a).
findings.location: Security section — 'refuse to start if any symmetric algorithm is configured … The symmetric set that triggers the startup refusal is the closed, explicitly-enumerated family {HS256, HS384, HS512} (case-insensitive); anything outside the asymmetric families RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA on a JWKS source is likewise refused.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: 'The closed asymmetric allowlist is: RS256, RS384, RS512, PS256, PS384, PS512, ES256, ES384, ES512, ES256K, EdDSA (matching PyJWT's supported asymmetric algorithms); any configured algorithm outside this set OR inside {HS256, HS384, HS512} causes startup refusal.'

findings.finding: The asyncio.Lock boundary for the unknown-kid cooldown is ambiguous in a way that leads to a correctness trap: if the Lock is held across `await asyncio.to_thread(get_signing_key_from_jwt)`, it serializes ALL concurrent token validations for the full HTTP timeout duration (up to 10 s), creating head-of-line blocking. If the Lock is released before the fetch (only protecting the timestamp update), then multiple concurrent to_thread calls for the same unknown kid proceed to call PyJWKClient concurrently — racing on PyJWKClient's internal cache dict, which is not documented as thread-safe. The plan's phrase 'serializes the check-and-update' is insufficient to specify which boundary is correct.
findings.location: Security section — 'Key rotation without DoS (concurrency-safe)' paragraph; also Approach & rationale — 'asyncio.Lock (see Security) so the at most one fetch per cooldown window guarantee holds'
findings.suggested_fix: The recommended pattern: (1) acquire Lock → check cooldown → update timestamp → release Lock → call await asyncio.to_thread(fetch_and_update_jwks_cache) outside the Lock. For thread-safety on cache hits, either document that PyJWKClient's internal dict is protected by the GIL (CPython-only assumption, acceptable but worth noting), or wrap PyJWKClient in a per-instance threading.Lock for all get_signing_key calls. Alternatively, hold the Lock across the to_thread call only when a refetch is needed, and use a separate (non-locking) path for cache-hit known-kid lookups.

findings.finding: The `scope`/`scp`→scopes claim-mapping protocol is referenced in the What section but the merge/priority rule for when BOTH claims are simultaneously present in a JWT is never defined. This is a missing decision rule for a named processing protocol (T8b).
findings.location: What section — 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Claims are resolved in order: `scope` (space-delimited string) is checked first; if absent, `scp` (space-delimited string or list) is used; if both are present, `scope` takes precedence and `scp` is ignored.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the cooldown concurrency AC oracle from 'request count ≤1' to 'request count == 1' (exactly one fetch in the burst window) to distinguish correct capped-refetch behavior from a broken no-fetch path.
evidence: "the stateful JWKS fake's request count is ≤1 for the burst" — ≤1 includes 0
A deadlock or over-aggressive lock on the refetch path would produce 0 fetches, satisfying ≤1 while being a bug
The rotation sub-assertion ('a legitimate rotation still refetches') only covers post-cooldown, not the burst's minimum count
findings.finding: The cooldown concurrency AC's observable oracle 'JWKS fake's request count is ≤1 for the burst' is satisfied by 0 fetches (no fetch at all), which would indicate the lock is preventing ALL fetches rather than capping to exactly one. The oracle should assert exactly 1 fetch (not merely ≤1) to distinguish correct behavior from a broken implementation that never fetches.
findings.location: AC: 'Unit test asserts unknown-`kid` cooldown is concurrency-safe: ... request count is ≤1 for the burst'
scenarios: A buggy implementation that acquires the lock and never releases it, or simply never calls the JWKS endpoint during the burst, passes the ≤1 oracle while being functionally broken for unknown-kid recovery.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC text to: 'the stateful JWKS fake's request count equals exactly 1 for the burst (at least one fetch triggered, at most one — the asyncio.Lock-guarded check-and-update prevents TOCTOU double-fetch).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `JWTVerifier` exists in the installed `mcp` SDK (or document the actual FastMCP auth seam interface — e.g. the base class or protocol `JWKSTokenVerifier` must implement), then update the Approach section to reference the correct symbol/interface rather than the unverifiable 'FastMCP's JWTVerifier' claim.
evidence: resolve_symbol JWTVerifier in mcp.server.fastmcp → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol JWTVerifier in mcp.server.auth → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol JWTVerifier in mcp.server.fastmcp.server → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol JWTVerifier in mcp.server.auth.handlers → UNRESOLVED

This is an empirically-checkable codebase/SDK assertion that cannot be verified.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses'
scenarios: The design shape claimed to mirror FastMCP's JWTVerifier is actually the author's own design, not validated prior art — if FastMCP's actual auth seam has a different interface (e.g., a different verify_token signature or middleware hook), the implementation could be incompatible with FastMCP's auth integration points
An executor treats 'same shape as JWTVerifier' as a guidance to look up FastMCP's JWTVerifier for interface reference, finds nothing, and must guess the interface

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve whether `FastMCP.JWTVerifier` (or equivalent) exists in the installed `mcp` SDK by locating its source, and either (a) cite the actual class path if it exists, or (b) remove the reference and replace it with the actual MCP auth seam interface that `JWKSTokenVerifier` must implement.
evidence: Plan text: 'PyJWT's documented JWKS path and the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses, giving provider-generic OIDC validation in a small module on the SDK seam'
resolve_symbol JWTVerifier in mcp.server.auth → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol JWTVerifier in mcp.server.fastmcp → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol JWTVerifier in mcp → UNRESOLVED
findings.finding: The plan cites 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses' as design-validation prior art for the chosen `PyJWKClient` + `jwt.decode` approach. `JWTVerifier` is UNRESOLVED in every reachable `mcp` namespace (`mcp`, `mcp.server.fastmcp`, `mcp.server.auth`). If the class does not exist in the installed SDK the citation is baseless, the design lacks its stated prior-art grounding, and any structural assumptions borrowed from a nonexistent reference may be incorrect.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section ('the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses')
scenarios: The executor searches for `JWTVerifier` to understand the expected interface shape and cannot find it, leading to inconsistent integration assumptions.

The cited prior art does not exist, meaning the design's structural choices lack validation against an established reference, and any divergence from the actual MCP auth seam is undetected until integration testing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` (or whichever module owns the `mcp.auth_strategies` dispatch) as an explicit edit target and describe the wiring change needed to select `JWKSTokenVerifier` for the `jwt` strategy.
evidence: Plan: 'selected by the `jwt` token in `mcp.auth_strategies`' implies a dispatch table or conditional in the server assembly path
`build_server()` in `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` lines 241-270 is the sole server assembler; it currently has no auth-strategy dispatch
Plan lists only `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` as the implementation file; `mcp_server.py` is not mentioned
findings.finding: `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` (specifically `build_server()`) is an unenumerated edit target: the plan states the verifier is 'selected by the `jwt` token in `mcp.auth_strategies`', which requires wiring logic inside `build_server()` or an auth-dispatch layer that `build_server()` invokes — but `mcp_server.py` is never listed as a file the story will touch.
findings.location: Scope section / What section ('selected by the `jwt` token in `mcp.auth_strategies`')
scenarios: An implementer completes `_mcp_auth.py` in full but has no plan-specified location for the wiring code that makes the `jwt` strategy key invoke `JWKSTokenVerifier` — the dispatch point is left to inference

findings.finding: The plan never specifies the verifier's behavior when a JWT has no `kid` header claim. PyJWKClient raises an exception in this case by default, but whether the verifier should treat this as 'definitively invalid → return None' or 'fetch error → raise' is unspecified — a decision point with a security-relevant outcome (T8d).
findings.location: Security section — 'Key rotation without DoS'; AC — 'a JWKS-fetch failure raises … distinct from a definitively-invalid token which returns None'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'A JWT with no `kid` header is treated as definitively invalid → return None (not a JWKS-fetch failure), since the key cannot be selected regardless of JWKS state.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/env-vars.md` (regenerated via `scripts/gen_env_registry.py`) to the list of artifacts this story touches, and include running `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` in the AC or Compatibility notes alongside the existing `check_server_manifest.py` step.
evidence: Plan mentions 'regenerates the reference docs' but does not name `docs/env-vars.md` or `scripts/gen_env_registry.py`
`scripts/gen_env_registry.py` scans `src/rebar/**/*.py` for `os.environ` reads and writes `docs/env-vars.md`; its CI gate is separate from the `check_server_manifest.py` gate named in the AC
`tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` line 30: 'env-var registry drift gate': 'scripts/gen_env_registry.py'
`gen_env_registry.py` is wired into `.github/workflows/test.yml` (line 260) and `gerrit-verify.yaml` (line 219)
findings.finding: `docs/env-vars.md` — generated by `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` via AST-scanning of `os.environ` reads — is an unenumerated consumer artifact. New `auth_jwt_*` env reads in `_mcp_auth.py` will change the generated output, requiring `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` to be run and committed; a CI drift gate (wired in both `.github/workflows/test.yml` and `gerrit-verify.yaml`) will fail if the file is stale.
findings.location: What section ('registers its keys in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json + regenerates the reference docs') / AC line 2
scenarios: `make test` passes locally because the implementer ran `check_server_manifest.py` but forgot `gen_env_registry.py`; the CI env-var registry drift gate then fails on the PR
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC line 2: 'and `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` regenerates `docs/env-vars.md` with the new keys (CI env-var registry drift gate passes).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that asserts a JWT carrying only the `scp` claim (not `scope`) is accepted and its scopes are correctly mapped to `AccessToken.scopes` (observable oracle: `AccessToken.scopes` equals the `scp` array).
evidence: "maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes" — stated in What
ABSENCE: no AC specifies an observable oracle for a token carrying only `scp` (not `scope`) being mapped to the expected scopes
AC for happy path only says 'correct scopes' without distinguishing the claim name used
findings.finding: The 'What' section specifies 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes' (dual-claim scope mapping with `scp` as a fallback), but no acceptance criterion covers the `scp` claim path. An executor can ship a verifier that only reads the `scope` claim and silently drops `scp`-only tokens without failing any AC.
findings.location: What section ('maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes') vs. AC block (no AC for `scp` fallback)
scenarios: An IdP that issues `scp`-claim tokens (e.g., some Azure AD configurations) gets no scope mapping, silently returning an empty scope set, but all ACs pass green.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC block: '[ ] Unit test asserts a JWT with only the `scp` claim (no `scope` claim) produces an `AccessToken` whose `.scopes` equal the `scp` value, while a JWT with both `scope` and `scp` uses `scope` (observable oracle: scopes match expected list in each case).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that verifies `asyncio.to_thread` (or equivalent non-blocking dispatch) is used for the JWKS fetch — e.g., patch `asyncio.to_thread` to assert it is called, or assert that the event loop is not blocked during a slow (artificially delayed) JWKS response.
evidence: 'The verifier therefore offloads every key lookup / JWKS fetch to a worker thread via asyncio.to_thread(...) so it never blocks the event loop'
ABSENCE: no AC text mentions asyncio.to_thread, run_in_executor, or blocking-call detection
findings.finding: The Approach section describes offloading every JWKS fetch / key lookup to `asyncio.to_thread()` to prevent event-loop blocking — a mandatory correctness property for an async server — but no acceptance criterion verifies this behavior. An implementer could satisfy all ACs while calling `PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt()` directly on the event loop (blocking), and no test would catch it.
findings.location: Approach section ('offloads every key lookup / JWKS fetch to a worker thread via asyncio.to_thread(...)') vs. Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)
scenarios: An implementer forgets the to_thread wrapper; all existing ACs still pass (AC #9 tests the lock/cooldown, not the threading), but the event loop is blocked under load.
A code reviewer has no test to point at when verifying the non-blocking guarantee.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: 'Unit test asserts that JWKS key resolution is dispatched via asyncio.to_thread (observable oracle: asyncio.to_thread is called during verify_token, e.g., by patching it and asserting call count ≥ 1 per verification).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that explicitly tests the `scp` claim variant: a JWT with `scp` (space-separated scopes, no `scope` claim) produces the expected scopes in `AccessToken`, and a JWT with `scope` produces the same result (both variants exercised).
evidence: 'maps scope/scp→scopes' (What section)
AC #3: 'a JWT with correct iss/aud(=resource) → an AccessToken with the expected scopes' — does not specify which claim field is used or test the scp variant
findings.finding: The What section states the verifier 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes' (supporting both the standard `scope` claim and the Microsoft-style `scp` claim), but no acceptance criterion tests the `scp` variant. AC #3 only tests the happy path with correct `iss`/`aud`, leaving the `scp` mapping completely untested.
findings.location: What section ('maps scope/scp→scopes') vs. Acceptance Criteria #3
scenarios: An implementer only handles `scope` and ignores `scp`; all ACs pass but Microsoft Entra ID tokens (which use `scp`) silently return no scopes.
The `scp` branch is implemented but has a bug (e.g., wrong split character); no test catches it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: JWKS fetch failures and timeouts MUST emit a structured log entry (at WARNING or ERROR level, redacted) distinct from token-validation failures, so operators can distinguish JWKS-endpoint health issues from invalid-token traffic.
evidence: 'All verify-path errors go through the S2 redaction helper so no token/secret substring is ever logged'
No AC or plan text mentions a log line, metric counter, or alert for JWKS fetch failures or verification errors.
ABSENCE: no mention of structured error logging, Prometheus counter, or tracing span for the JWKS fetch path.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for JWKS-fetch failures: the plan says errors go through the S2 redaction helper (to prevent token leakage), but does not specify any metric, structured log entry, or alert for JWKS fetch failures, timeouts, or unexpected verification errors — operators cannot distinguish a JWKS-endpoint outage from normal 401 traffic.
findings.location: Security section — last bullet; Acceptance Criteria — no observability AC
scenarios: JWKS endpoint goes down; all JWT-authenticated requests fail with 401; operator has no visibility into whether the problem is a bad token, an expired cert, or a JWKS endpoint outage — MTTR is high.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Establish and document PyJWKClient's thread-safety guarantee, or extend the asyncio.Lock to serialize ALL calls to `client.get_signing_key_from_jwt()` (not just the cooldown timestamp update), and add a unit test that exercises concurrent verification to confirm no data race.
evidence: 'the verifier therefore offloads every key lookup / JWKS fetch to a worker thread via asyncio.to_thread(...)'
'serializes the cooldown check-and-update with an asyncio.Lock'
The Lock is explicitly scoped to the cooldown check-and-update, not to the PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt() call itself.
ABSENCE: no assertion that PyJWKClient is thread-safe or that a broader lock/wrapper is used for all calls to it.
findings.finding: `PyJWKClient` is a shared singleton called concurrently from multiple worker threads (each `asyncio.to_thread` dispatch runs in a separate thread-pool thread), but the plan's `asyncio.Lock` only guards the cooldown-timestamp check-and-update — it does not serialize all calls to `PyJWKClient`. PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` is not documented as thread-safe; its internal JWKS cache (a dict) can be mutated mid-read by a concurrent fetch, producing a data race under concurrent requests.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — async-safety section; Security — Key rotation without DoS
scenarios: Two concurrent requests with known kids both call asyncio.to_thread(client.get_signing_key_from_jwt) in parallel; simultaneously a third request triggers a JWKS refetch that mutates the client's internal cache dict — Python's GIL protects individual bytecodes but not compound dict operations during a refetch, risking a KeyError or stale-key read.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the entire `asyncio.to_thread(client.get_signing_key_from_jwt, token)` call inside the `asyncio.Lock` (or a dedicated per-client lock), so only one thread accesses the PyJWKClient at a time. Alternatively, verify from PyJWT's source/docs that PyJWKClient is thread-safe and cite that in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs that behaviorally test `auth_jwt_leeway`: (1) a token expired within the configured leeway window is accepted, and (2) a token expired beyond the leeway window is rejected, with distinct observable oracles for each.
evidence: Absence: no AC mentions `auth_jwt_leeway` in any test scenario.

Security section: '`auth_jwt_leeway` (seconds, default 60)' is listed as a key the verifier uses for `exp`/`nbf` with bounded leeway, but the only `exp`-related AC is 'expired `exp`' with no leeway nuance.

The `exp`/`nbf` rejection AC does not specify whether it tests at-exactly-expired vs. expired-beyond-leeway, meaning a misconfigured leeway could silently make the 'expired exp' test pass by coincidence.
findings.finding: The `auth_jwt_leeway` parameter is a security-relevant configurable value (controls how many seconds past expiry a token is still accepted) but no AC tests its behavioral effect: there is no case asserting that a token expired within the leeway window is accepted, or that a token expired beyond the leeway is rejected even under the leeway setting. A broken or ignored leeway implementation would pass all current ACs.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — no AC covers leeway; Security section defines `auth_jwt_leeway` (default 60s)
scenarios: A bug where leeway is applied as a negative offset (expanding rejection window) or not applied at all would be undetected.
An operator sets leeway to 0 for strictness — no test confirms this tightens the window as expected.
findings.suggested_fix: Add two ACs: 'Unit test: with leeway=60, a token whose exp is 30 s in the past is accepted (→ AccessToken); with leeway=0 the same token is rejected (→ None).' Use a locally-minted token with a controlled exp timestamp so the oracle is deterministic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs for scope/scp edge cases: (1) token with no scope/scp claim → AccessToken with empty scopes, (2) token with scp-only claim → correct scopes extracted, (3) token with both scope and scp → deterministic priority rule applied and verified.
evidence: 'maps scope/scp→scopes' is listed in the What section as a behavior the verifier performs.
Happy-path AC: 'a JWT with correct iss/aud(=resource) → an AccessToken with the expected scopes' — covers only the populated-scope case.
Absence: no AC tests scope=absent, scope='', scp-only (no scope claim), or both scope+scp present simultaneously.
findings.finding: The `scope`/`scp` claim mapping is described as new behavior ("maps scope/scp→scopes"), but the only AC covering it is the happy-path success case ("expected scopes"). No AC tests the absent/empty scope path (many real access tokens have no scope claim, or an empty string scope), the scp-only path, or the priority when both scope and scp are present. These are realistic production inputs with user-visible effects (scope-gated access decisions).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Unit test for happy path (AC line 3); 'maps scope/scp→scopes' in What section
scenarios: A token with no scope claim (common for machine-to-machine tokens that are validated by audience alone) yields an AccessToken with unexpected scopes or raises instead of returning an empty scope set.
When both scope and scp are present, the priority rule is untested — a bug where scope shadows scp or vice versa would be invisible.
An empty-string scope claim producing a non-empty scope set (e.g. ['']) could cause incorrect access grants downstream.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether PyJWKClient is thread-safe under concurrent to_thread calls, and if not, specify that either (a) all calls to get_signing_key_from_jwt are serialized under the asyncio.Lock (expanding its scope) or (b) a per-request PyJWKClient instance is used, or (c) a threading.Lock inside the shared client is used.
evidence: Plan: 'the cooldown's read-check-then-update of the last-refetch timestamp is … made atomic under an asyncio.Lock that serializes the check-and-update'
The Lock scope is narrowly the timestamp update — it does NOT wrap the to_thread call itself
Happy-path concurrent requests (kid in cache) call asyncio.to_thread(get_signing_key_from_jwt) in parallel with no serialization, all sharing one PyJWKClient instance
PyJWT's PyJWKClient is not documented as thread-safe in the installed version (site-packages/jwt/__init__.py)
findings.finding: The asyncio.Lock guards only the cooldown timestamp check-and-update, but PyJWKClient's internal key cache is shared mutable state that is raced by all concurrent asyncio.to_thread(get_signing_key_from_jwt) calls on the happy path (cached kid). PyJWT's PyJWKClient is not documented as thread-safe; its internal jwk_set cache is a mutable dict mutated during fetches and lookups. Under concurrent requests where the kid is already cached, multiple threads call into the same PyJWKClient instance simultaneously with no serialization, creating a potential race on the shared cache state that the plan's Lock design does not address.
findings.location: Approach & rationale / Security — 'Key rotation without DoS (concurrency-safe)' / 'asyncio.Lock that serializes the check-and-update'
scenarios: High-traffic burst: 50 concurrent valid-kid requests all hit asyncio.to_thread(client.get_signing_key_from_jwt) simultaneously, racing on PyJWKClient's internal jwk_set dict
Key rotation event where two concurrent threads race a JWKS fetch and a cache read simultaneously
findings.suggested_fix: Expand the asyncio.Lock to cover the entire get_signing_key_from_jwt call (not just the timestamp check-and-update) — or confirm in the plan that PyJWKClient's internal cache is thread-safe. Alternatively, instantiate one PyJWKClient per verify_token call (stateless per-request, no shared cache race; accept re-fetching as the cost). The simplest correct fix: hold the asyncio.Lock for the full await asyncio.to_thread(...) call, since the JWKS fetch is the slow path (already guarded by cooldown) and the fast path (cache hit) is fast enough that brief serialization is not a throughput problem.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (by inspecting the installed `PyJWKClient.__init__` signature) that `timeout=` is the correct constructor kwarg for HTTP fetch timeout in the pinned PyJWT version, and document the exact kwarg name in the plan; if incorrect, specify the right mechanism (e.g. `request_timeout=`, a custom `RequestsSession`, or a wrapper).
evidence: Plan text: 'with a bounded HTTP timeout given by the named key `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (seconds, default `10`), passed as the `timeout=` argument to `PyJWKClient` so a slow/hung JWKS endpoint cannot stall the verifier'
AC#7: 'the JWKS client is constructed with `timeout=auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (default `10` s) (observable oracle: … the concrete timeout value is passed to the client)'
`jwt.PyJWKClient` EXISTS in installed env but constructor signature is unresolved from plan text alone
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'passed as the `timeout=` argument to `PyJWKClient`' as a verifiable capability, but PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` constructor keyword for HTTP timeout is not confirmed in the installed version. Historical PyJWT releases used `lifespan=` for cache TTL and did not expose a `timeout=` kwarg directly; newer versions may use `headers=` or configure timeout through a separate mechanism. AC#7 makes this observable ('the concrete timeout value is passed to the client'), so a wrong kwarg name silently disables the timeout without failing the AC.
findings.location: Security section ('bounded HTTP timeout … `timeout=` argument to `PyJWKClient`') and AC#7
scenarios: Executor passes `timeout=10` to PyJWKClient but the kwarg is silently ignored (or raises TypeError) because the actual parameter name differs — the JWKS fetch becomes unbounded, contradicting the SSRF-safety guarantee.
AC#7 passes if the test only checks the kwarg name string rather than observing actual timeout behavior, masking the bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Inspect the installed PyJWT: `import inspect, jwt; print(inspect.signature(jwt.PyJWKClient.__init__))`. If `timeout` is not a parameter, use the approach documented in PyJWT's source (e.g. subclass or `cache_jwk_set=True` + a custom fetcher) and update the AC to match the real API.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the installed PyJWT version's `PyJWKClient.__init__` accepts a `timeout=` constructor kwarg (flag-level check against `--help`/source/docs), and if not, specify the correct API surface for passing a fetch timeout (e.g. a custom `get_jwk_data` callable wrapping `requests.get(..., timeout=N)`, or a monkey-patched session).
evidence: Plan: 'with a bounded HTTP timeout given by the named key auth_jwt_jwks_timeout (seconds, default 10), passed as the timeout= argument to PyJWKClient so a slow/hung JWKS endpoint cannot stall the verifier'
resolve_symbol(PyJWKClient, jwt.jwks_client) → EXISTS, but the constructor's `timeout=` kwarg cannot be confirmed from available tooling
Known PyJWKClient constructor surface (from prior cache analysis in this repo): uri, lifespan, get_jwk_data, jwks_data — no timeout= confirmed
Evidence class: Unverified (specific constructor parameter surface — flag-level mismatch risk)
findings.finding: The plan asserts the JWKS fetch timeout is applied by passing `timeout=auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` as a constructor argument to `PyJWKClient`, but this specific kwarg is unverified in the installed PyJWT version. PyJWKClient's documented constructor parameters are `uri`, `lifespan`, `get_jwk_data`, and `jwks_data`; `timeout=` at the constructor level is not confirmed. If this kwarg does not exist, the timeout is silently dropped — nullifying the primary SSRF-safe fetch bound the Security section depends on.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch'; AC line 7 ('auth_jwt_jwks_timeout … passed as the `timeout=` argument to `PyJWKClient`')
scenarios: Implementor writes PyJWKClient(uri=jwks_uri, timeout=cfg.jwks_timeout) — if timeout= is not accepted, the kwarg is silently ignored (or raises TypeError) and a hung IdP endpoint blocks the asyncio worker thread indefinitely

The AC asserts an observable oracle ('the concrete timeout value is passed to the client') — if the param does not exist, this AC cannot pass without a workaround, forcing late-stage rework of the timeout threading approach
findings.suggested_fix: If `timeout=` is not a valid PyJWKClient constructor kwarg in the installed version, pass a custom `get_jwk_data=` callable to `PyJWKClient` that wraps `requests.get(uri, timeout=cfg.jwks_timeout).json()` — this is the documented extensibility seam for controlling the underlying HTTP call. Update the plan's Security section and AC 7 to name this mechanism explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `JWTVerifier` (or its equivalent) exists in the installed `mcp>=1.9` SDK (check `mcp.server.auth` submodules), and either update the Approach section with the correct symbol/path or remove the reference if it no longer exists in this SDK version.
evidence: Plan: 'PyJWT's documented JWKS path and the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
resolve_symbol(JWTVerifier, mcp.server.auth) → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol(JWTVerifier, mcp) → UNRESOLVED
mcp>=1.9 is the installed version per pyproject.toml
Evidence class: Unverified (the specific symbol is not importable from the installed SDK)
findings.finding: The Approach section cites 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses' as design-validation prior art, implying `JWTVerifier` exists as a resolvable symbol in the installed MCP/FastMCP SDK. All resolution probes of `mcp.server.auth.JWTVerifier` and `mcp.JWTVerifier` return UNRESOLVED. If `JWTVerifier` does not exist in the installed SDK version, the stated design analogy is ungrounded, and any executor who searches for it as a reference implementation will find nothing.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section — 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
scenarios: An executor searching for JWTVerifier in the mcp package as a reference implementation finds nothing, losing the intended design anchor
If JWTVerifier was removed or renamed in mcp>=1.9, the analogy comparison is stale and may point to a different (non-applicable) API shape

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the SSRF guard to validate the *resolved IP address* at fetch time, not only the hostname string at construction — e.g., resolve the hostname once at construction and pin the IP for the lifetime of the client, or use a custom urllib opener/transport that extracts the connected socket's peer address and applies the private-range check before consuming the response.
evidence: 'jwks_uri is fixed at construction from trusted config (never the token — no jku/x5u)' and 'Private/link-local hosts … are rejected by default' — the plan specifies only a construction-time string check on the URI hostname.
PyJWKClient is built on urllib (synchronous) and resolves DNS at each HTTP request independently; no plan text specifies a post-resolution IP address validation step.
The plan explicitly classifies SSRF protection as a 'hard requirement (RFC 8725 JWT BCP)' — making the construction-time-only check a material gap against its own declared standard.
DNS TTLs for IdP hostnames (e.g. Okta, Auth0) are typically 60–300 s; an attacker who can influence DNS after server startup can redirect a cleared hostname to 169.254.169.254.
findings.finding: The SSRF guard for the JWKS fetch is applied only at construction time (hostname-string check on the configured `jwks_uri`), but PyJWKClient resolves DNS at each HTTP fetch using the system resolver. A DNS rebinding attack, DNS cache poisoning, BGP hijack, or CNAME change to an internal address after server startup can redirect a construction-time-cleared hostname to a private/cloud-metadata IP (e.g. `169.254.169.254` EC2 IMDS) at runtime, fully bypassing the plan's declared 'rejected by default' private-host protection.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch' bullet; AC line 'a private/link-local host is rejected by default and accepted only with auth_jwt_allow_private_jwks_host=true'
scenarios: Attacker poisons the DNS cache for the operator-configured IdP hostname so that subsequent JWKS fetches (triggered by unknown-kid cooldown or cache TTL expiry) resolve to 169.254.169.254, leaking EC2 IMDS IAM credentials via SSRF despite the construction-time check passing cleanly.
An IdP operator CNAMEs their publicly-cleared hostname to an internal address during a migration; the running verifier silently fetches JWKS from the internal host on each cache-miss.
findings.suggested_fix: At construction, resolve the `jwks_uri` hostname to its IP(s) and verify none fall in RFC 1918 / loopback / link-local ranges (unless `auth_jwt_allow_private_jwks_host=true`). Then pass the pre-resolved IP (or a custom socket-factory that repeats the check) to `PyJWKClient` so DNS rebinding cannot redirect subsequent fetches. Alternatively, add the post-resolution IP check to the `asyncio.to_thread` wrapper that calls `get_signing_key_from_jwt`, rejecting any response whose socket resolved to a private address.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either resolve `JWTVerifier` to a specific importable path in the installed mcp SDK (with module + symbol), replace it with an alternative verifiable prior-art reference, or remove the reference and describe the interface shape directly in the plan.
evidence: Plan text: 'PyJWT's documented JWKS path and the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses'
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp.server.auth') → UNRESOLVED
No file in the repo matches `JWTVerifier` via search.
findings.finding: The Approach section references 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses' as design-validation prior art, but `JWTVerifier` is unresolvable in the installed mcp/mcp.server.fastmcp/mcp.server.auth namespaces. An executor using this reference to validate their implementation shape against existing SDK prior art cannot find the referenced artifact.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section — 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses'
scenarios: An executor looking for `JWTVerifier` in the mcp SDK to understand the expected interface shape finds nothing, cannot validate their design, and may implement a divergent interface.
If `JWTVerifier` doesn't exist, the 'same shape' claim is unfalsifiable and misleading.

findings.finding: The `auth_jwt_leeway` config key is described as a functional validation parameter in the 'What' section (default 60 s, affects exp/nbf acceptance), but no Acceptance Criterion tests its observable effect — e.g., that a token expired by fewer seconds than the leeway value is accepted, or that leeway is actually passed to jwt.decode. This is an orphaned deliverable with no covering criterion.
findings.location: What section (auth_jwt_leeway) + Acceptance Criteria (no covering AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the negative-control AC block: 'a token whose exp is in the past by fewer seconds than auth_jwt_leeway is accepted; one expired beyond leeway is rejected.' This makes leeway a first-class tested parameter rather than an untested config knob.

findings.finding: The Approach section declares that the blocking `PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt()` call MUST be offloaded to a worker thread via `asyncio.to_thread()` to avoid blocking the event loop — an explicit design invariant — but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this property. An implementation that skips the offload would pass all existing ACs.
findings.location: Approach section ('offloads every key lookup / JWKS fetch to a worker thread via asyncio.to_thread(...)') + Acceptance Criteria (no covering AC)

findings.finding: The 'What' section states the verifier 'maps scope/scp→scopes', implying both claim name variants must be handled. AC3 asserts 'an AccessToken with the expected scopes' but uses a single happy-path token — it does not specify that both `scope` (space-delimited string) and `scp` (array, as used by Entra ID / some IdPs) claim names produce the correct scopes list. The `scp` path is an orphaned sub-deliverable.
findings.location: What section ('maps scope/scp→scopes') + AC3
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, specify two sub-cases: one JWT with a `scope: 'read write'` claim and one with `scp: ['read', 'write']`, asserting both yield `['read', 'write']` in the AccessToken.

findings.finding: The `auth_jwt_leeway` parameter is registered and named in the plan but has no AC that verifies it actually works — i.e., that a token with `nbf` slightly in the future (or `exp` slightly in the past) within the leeway window is *accepted*. The only leeway-adjacent AC (negative control for `nbf`) tests strict rejection without leeway, leaving the positive case unobservable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 4 (rejection controls) and the What / Security sections describing `auth_jwt_leeway`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: 'Unit test asserts that a token with nbf = now+30s is accepted when auth_jwt_leeway=60 (within window) and rejected when auth_jwt_leeway=10 (outside window), confirming the leeway parameter is passed to jwt.decode.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the JWKS endpoint wiring strategy for the integration test (e.g., 'the integration test configures `auth_jwt_jwks_uri` to point to an in-process httpx mock transport or a pytest-served local HTTP endpoint') so the executor doesn't have to invent the approach.
evidence: The unit test specifies 'a stateful fake serving a locally-generated key set' but the integration test AC gives no analogous description of JWKS endpoint wiring
The unit tests use PyJWKClient mocking/faking; the integration test presumably needs the real PyJWKClient to fetch from somewhere — the plan is silent on this
SSRF guards and timeout are constructor-time but their integration-test behavior depends on what URI is configured for the server under test
findings.finding: The integration test AC ('httpx ASGITransport asserts a valid JWKS-issued token → 200') does not specify how the JWKS endpoint is made available to the FastMCP server under test — whether the same in-process fake is reused, a local HTTP server is spun up, or the JWKS URI is patched — leaving the executor to invent the wiring. This is a defaultable gap, but the approach differs materially between options (in-process monkeypatching vs. a real local HTTP server) and affects whether the `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` and SSRF guards actually exercise in the integration path.
findings.location: AC: 'Integration test (httpx `ASGITransport`) asserts a valid JWKS-issued token → 200 and a garbage bearer → 401 end-to-end through the transport.'
scenarios: Executor chooses to monkeypatch PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt directly in the integration test, bypassing the HTTP-fetch code path entirely — the timeout and SSRF guard code is never exercised in integration.
Executor spins up a real local HTTP server, which correctly exercises the path but adds complexity not scoped in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the integration test AC: 'The JWKS endpoint is served by a pytest fixture that starts a local HTTP server (e.g., using `pytest-httpserver` or `uvicorn` in a thread) so the real PyJWKClient HTTP fetch path executes, confirming the SSRF guard and timeout wiring hold in the integration path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the three novel security/architecture decisions (SSRF guard rationale, symmetric-alg startup refusal, and asyncio.Lock cooldown design) in a persisted ADR or CLAUDE.md section so future maintainers cannot unknowingly undo them.
evidence: 'PyJWKClient's `lifespan` is a cache TTL, not an unknown-kid flood guard, so this verifier implements the unknown-kid refetch cooldown itself'
'Private/link-local hosts … are rejected by default'
'refuse to start if any symmetric algorithm is configured … alongside a JWKS source'
No mention of ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc in the What/Scope/Compatibility sections
findings.finding: The plan introduces three novel architectural decisions — (1) the SSRF guard with private-host blocking and HTTPS-only enforcement, (2) the symmetric-algorithm startup refusal to block RS256↔HS256 confusion, and (3) the custom unknown-`kid` cooldown with `asyncio.Lock` instead of relying on PyJWKClient's `lifespan` — all described only in the ephemeral ticket Security section. None of these decisions are captured in a persisted ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc, so future maintainers have no durable record of why these constraints exist and cannot safely relax them.
findings.location: Security section; plan-level (no ADR reference)
scenarios: A future maintainer sees the SSRF host-check and removes it as 'over-engineered', not knowing it blocks metadata-endpoint attacks.
A contributor adds HS256 support thinking it's an omission, triggering the confusion attack the startup guard was designed to prevent.
The asyncio.Lock is replaced with a simple timestamp check after a refactor that 'simplifies' the cooldown, reintroducing the TOCTOU race.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `## Architecture Decision Record` subsection (or a `docs/adr/S3-jwks-verifier.md` file) that records: (1) HTTPS-only + private-host SSRF guard and the attack it prevents, (2) the symmetric-alg startup refusal and the RS256↔HS256 confusion attack it blocks, and (3) why the cooldown is implemented in-verifier with asyncio.Lock rather than delegated to PyJWKClient's lifespan. Reference this doc from CLAUDE.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` needs to be a public operator config key (registered in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`/reference docs) or can instead be a module-level constant; if no operator scenario requires tuning it beyond the 30 s default, drop it from the public surface to avoid config-surface proliferation.
evidence: Plan What section: 'auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown (seconds, default 30)' listed alongside genuinely operator-facing keys like jwks_uri and issuer.
Plan Approach: 'implemented by the verifier itself, not PyJWKClient's lifespan' — this is an internal mechanism, not an operator-visible concern.
Plan Security: 'caps refetch on an unknown kid to at most one refetch per auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown seconds (default 30)' — the 30s default is sufficient for all standard key-rotation scenarios.
No operator scenario cited where the 30s default is wrong.
`MCP_ENV_VARS` in `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` is the single source of truth for operator-visible env vars; adding an internal implementation tunable proliferates the public surface unnecessarily.
findings.finding: `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` is exposed as a public operator config key (registered in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`, included in reference docs) for a purely internal JWKS refetch flood-guard parameter. The plan itself describes it as 'implemented by the verifier itself,' and no operator scenario is cited where the hardcoded 30s default is wrong. This is config-surface proliferation: adding a public tunable for an internal implementation detail without a concrete operator need.
findings.location: What section (flat config keys list) and Security section ('at most one refetch per `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` seconds')
scenarios: An executor adds the key to MCP_ENV_VARS, server.json, and the reference docs; operators discover it and worry about tuning it; future authors must maintain it in the drift-guard script forever — all for a parameter that has a correct default and no cited operator use case.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the refetch cooldown a private module constant (`_JWKS_REFETCH_COOLDOWN_S = 30`) inside `_mcp_auth.py`; omit it from `MCP_ENV_VARS`, `server.json`, and the regenerated reference docs. Promote it to a public key only when a concrete operator need is identified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between 'PyJWT is already a transitive of mcp' (Approach) and 'pyproject.toml adds pyjwt[crypto] to [mcp]' (AC-1): confirm whether pyjwt is currently a declared transitive of mcp>=1.9 (check mcp's published metadata), and if not, remove the 'already a transitive' framing and keep AC-1 as the authoritative add.

AC-1: 'pyproject.toml adds `pyjwt[crypto]` to `[mcp]`' — if it were a guaranteed transitive, this add would be unnecessary and the claim would be misleading
The 'transitive' claim is empirically checkable (inspect mcp's declared deps) but cannot be verified from the repo files available
findings.finding: The plan states 'PyJWT is already a transitive of mcp' to justify that the library is available, but pyproject.toml's [mcp] extra declares only 'mcp>=1.9' with no pyjwt dependency, and the plan's own AC-1 says 'pyproject.toml adds pyjwt[crypto] to [mcp]' — which is logically redundant if pyjwt were a guaranteed transitive. The 'already a transitive' claim is not verifiable from the repo, and the AC contradicts it by treating the add as work to be done.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`'; AC-1: 'pyproject.toml adds `pyjwt[crypto]` to `[mcp]`'
scenarios: A future `mcp` release drops the pyjwt transitive (if it ever was one), silently breaking the verifier in deployments that only install `nava-rebar[mcp]` without the explicit pyjwt add
An executor reads 'already a transitive' and omits the explicit pyjwt[crypto] add from pyproject.toml, leaving the cryptography backend (RS256/ES256) uninstalled
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`' with 'Add `pyjwt[crypto]` explicitly to the `[mcp]` extra (it may be a transitive today but is not a declared dep of `mcp`, so an explicit pin is required for stability).' This makes AC-1 unambiguously load-bearing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document in the Approach section why async-native JWKS client libraries (e.g. `authlib.jose`, `joserfc`, or a future `jwt.AsyncJWKClient`) were considered and rejected in favour of the `PyJWKClient` + `asyncio.to_thread` + `asyncio.Lock` composed pattern, satisfying the T1 prior-art/anti-repackaging check for this novel architecture.
evidence: Plan text: 'The verifier therefore offloads every key lookup / JWKS fetch to a worker thread via `asyncio.to_thread(...)` … serializes the cooldown check-and-update with an `asyncio.Lock`'
No mention of async-native JWKS libraries (authlib, joserfc, python-jose) as alternatives considered and rejected
resolve_symbol AsyncJWKClient in jwt → UNRESOLVED (not proof of absence, but not confirmed present either)
The Lock-held-across-await pattern is a known correctness hazard that would not arise with a native async client
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel composed pattern — synchronous `PyJWKClient` + `asyncio.to_thread` offload + `asyncio.Lock` serialization for the unknown-kid refetch cooldown — without considering whether an async-native JWKS client would make the entire bespoke design unnecessary. PyJWT's `AsyncJWKClient` is UNRESOLVED in the installed version, but `authlib` and `joserfc` both expose async JWKS paths natively. The Rule-of-Three / anti-repackaging check is not done: the plan constructs a novel multi-component pattern without documenting why async alternatives were rejected.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section and Security / 'Async-safety of the blocking JWKS fetch'
scenarios: An async-native JWKS client exists (or becomes available in a PyJWT update) that would eliminate the thread-offload + lock composition entirely, making the bespoke pattern a maintenance liability.

The asyncio.Lock-guarded check-and-update held across `await asyncio.to_thread(...)` introduces subtle correctness issues that a reviewer must reason about carefully but which are never compared against the simpler async alternative.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph rationale in Approach: PyJWT is already a transitive (no new dep), `authlib`/`joserfc` would add a new dependency and a different API surface, and the thread-offload pattern is consistent with how other sync-library integrations are handled in the codebase. This grounds the choice without needing to change the design.

findings.finding: AC 3's observable oracle for `expires_at` — 'a positive `expires_at`' — does not verify that the field is set to the token's actual `exp` claim value, allowing a buggy implementation that sets `expires_at` to any positive constant (e.g., 1) to satisfy the criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 3: 'a positive `expires_at` (observable oracle: returned `AccessToken` fields)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC to: '…a positive `expires_at` equal to the `exp` claim of the issued test token (assert `access_token.expires_at == test_jwt_exp`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that verifies error-path log output does not contain token substrings: e.g., assert that when verification fails with a known token value, the captured log output does not contain that token string (using the S2 redaction helper).
evidence: 'All verify-path errors go through the S2 redaction helper so no token/secret substring is ever logged'
ABSENCE: no AC mentions log output, redaction, or the S2 redaction helper
findings.finding: The Security section states 'All verify-path errors go through the S2 redaction helper so no token/secret substring is ever logged,' but no acceptance criterion verifies that error-path log output is redacted. This is a stated security requirement with no observable oracle in the ACs.
findings.location: Security section (last bullet) vs. Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)
scenarios: An implementer omits the redaction helper call on one error branch (e.g., the JWKS fetch failure path); a token substring leaks into logs; no AC catches it.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
